comments: This paper proposes learning multilingual multimodal embeddings by using images as a pivot between languages. The authors introduce two related architectures (PIVOT and PARALLEL) and test symmetric (cosine) and asymmetric (order) similarity functions, showing improvements on image–description retrieval and semantic textual similarity (STS) tasks using the Multi30k dataset (English–German). The idea of using an image as a bridge between non-parallel descriptions is well motivated and experimentally validated; the paper shows particularly strong gains for German, plausibly due to sparse German lexical coverage. Strengths: clear problem statement, simple and well-motivated loss formulations, experiments on established benchmarks, and investigation of symmetric vs. asymmetric scoring. Weaknesses and areas for improvement: experiments are limited to two languages (English/German) and a single dataset; comparisons to some related multilingual multimodal work are incomplete (authors explain differences but direct comparable evaluations would strengthen claims); ablation studies could be deeper (e.g., effect of minibatch negative sampling strategy, sensitivity to margin and embedding size, and the contribution of the PARALLEL term beyond PIVOT); discussion of computational cost and training stability is missing. The asymmetric-order similarity variant appears useful but would benefit from more analysis explaining when and why it helps. I recommend acceptance as a solid contribution to multilingual multimodal representation learning, suitable for presentation as a poster (or short oral slot), with suggested future extensions including scaling to more languages/datasets, stronger baselines and ablations, and more analysis of cross-lingual transfer dynamics.
